evidence: more insurers are utilizing or may begin utilizing big data analytics or artificial intelligence (AI) to make underwriting or other decisions that impact product design and pricing
When new technologies, such as AI, are incorporated into CNA s or its third-party service providers processes, they may introduce additional complexity
If such utilization by CNA s industry peers is more effective than how it uses its data and information, including through its own use of AI, CNA will be at a competitive disadvantage
the increasing use of AI, within CNA s systems and those of its vendors and third-party administrators to achieve operational efficiencies
generative AI systems may hallucinate producing inaccurate or misleading information, and model performance may degrade over time, leading to flawed recommendations
AI models may perpetuate or amplify biases present in underlying data, which could result in discriminatory or unfair outcomes in areas such as underwriting and claims

potential for employees or third-party service providers, through intentional or inadvertent actions, to enable AI models to be trained on CNA s data or its insureds data introduces risks of unauthorized use

limitations: No quantified AI investment amounts, headcount, or dedicated AI budget disclosed in the excerpts; investment intensity scored 2 based on generic competitive framing only.
AI narrative concreteness is aspirational/risk-framed throughout; no named deployed AI initiatives or production metrics found in excerpts.

Risk disclosure depth is high and company-specific with named mechanisms (hallucination, bias, data privacy, fraud), but no quantified risk figures or named governance bodies appear in these excerpts, limiting score to 4 rather than 5.
Boardwalk Pipelines references to AI data centers as demand drivers are market-context observations, not investment commitments by Loews/Boardwalk itself.
